pass1_value.note: The SQL API documentation explicitly describes OAuth 2.0 and key-pair (JWT/private-key) authentication and usage of bearer tokens. 'api_key' and 'token' included as general labels because the docs show use of bearer tokens (access tokens) and key-pair credentials registered for API Integration. See the cited page for exact supported flows.

final_value.note: Snowflake SQL API docs list supported methods as OAuth (using an OAuth access token) and key-pair authentication (sign a JWT). Requests use an Authorization: Bearer <token> header where the bearer token is either an OAuth access token or a JWT.

pass1_value.note: Snowflake offers a public free trial sign-up flow allowing developers to create accounts and obtain credentials without human approval; combined with docs showing how to create API_INTEGRATION objects inside an account, this supports self-serve credential setup.

final_value.note: Snowflake's official Get Started page advertises a self-serve free trial sign-up ("Start for free" / "Start your free trial"), indicating developers can create an account without sales approval to obtain access for building/testing.

pass1_value.note: Developer-facing REST SQL API docs exist and a public free trial sign-up is available, so a developer can obtain credentials and begin integrating immediately. Production usage may require paid account/consumption (per pricing), but that does not prevent building and testing.

final_value.note: Snowflake provides public SQL API documentation including supported authentication (OAuth access token or key-pair/JWT bearer token) and offers a self-serve free trial sign-up flow, so a developer can obtain an account and start integrating immediately (with potential paid usage later depending on consumption).
